text: The laparoscopic procedure involves skeletonizing the hilum of the left hepatic pedicle. The gallbladder is retracted upwards, and a small hemangioma on the right side is excised separately.

The surgeon clips and divides the cystic artery, followed by controlling the left hepatic artery with ligatures. The hemangioma appears smaller after double clipping and division. The dissection progresses deeper into the hilum, where the left portal vein is visualized, circumferentially dissected, and doubly ligated. Smaller parenchymal communications are sealed and divided.

The surgeon mobilizes the left lobe of the liver by dividing the falciform ligament towards the hepatic islets. The hemangioma appears reduced in size. The falciform ligament is divided on both sides. The hepatic duct is clipped and divided, followed by the division of the cystic duct and separation of the gallbladder. A stitch is placed at the right side of segment V, and the principal plane of the liver is marked with a harmonic scalpel, extending the margin cranially.

The surgeon clips and divides the vessels and ducts, continuing the dissection cranially. The left hepatic vein is identified, and a bleeding vein is initially clipped and then sutured. The left triangular ligament is completely mobilized from the left lobe to the origin of the left hepatic vein. A stapler is used across the parenchyma to divide the vein.

The segment is completely separated, and the hepatic duct on the left side is clearly visualized and either clipped or sutured. Organoplasma coagulation is used for hemostasis during the final inspection. The gallbladder, initially used for traction, is separated, and the specimen is removed through a Pfannenstiel incision.
